summary: DHH breaks down Basecamp's $3 million annual operations budget to show that only 15% ($450,000) is spent on actually running Ruby on Rails app and job servers. The vast majority goes to infrastructure like file storage, databases, search, and caching that would be needed regardless of programming language. Even if a hypothetical technology were twice as fast, the savings ($225,000) would be trivial compared to developer salaries. He argues that making the 15-person developer team even 15% less productive by switching languages would cost more than the entire Ruby/Rails compute budget. The conclusion is that for most SaaS companies, choosing a programming language based on raw performance is economically irrational.
key_points: File storage is Basecamp's largest operations cost at $1M/year for 4.5 petabytes, dwarfing compute costs
Most infrastructure spend (databases, search, caching, storage) is language-agnostic and would exist regardless of tech stack
Even a 2x performance improvement would only save $225,000/year — less than Basecamp's annual employee Christmas gift
Developer productivity matters far more than compute efficiency: a 15% productivity loss would exceed the entire Ruby/Rails compute budget
For most SaaS companies, the programming language and framework represent a small minority of total operations cost
money_quotes: So against a $3 million budget, it's about $450,000. That's it.
We spend more money than that on the Xmas gift we give employees at Basecamp every year.
That reduction wouldn't even pay for two developers at our average all-in cost at Basecamp!
If we make that team just 15% less productive, it'll cost us more than everything we spend to run Ruby and Rails at Basecamp!

For a company like Basecamp, you'd be mad to make your choice of programming language and web framework on anything but a determination of what'll make your programmers the most motivated, happy, and productive.
Whatever the cost, it's worth it. It's worth it on a pure cost/benefit, but, more importantly, it's worth it in terms of human happiness and potential.
This is why we run Ruby. This is why we run Rails. It's a complete bargain.
key_insight: For most SaaS companies, developer happiness and productivity dwarf compute costs, making programming language performance benchmarks economically irrelevant to the tech stack decision.
